agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform adding real-time driver earnings transparency across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications, legal requirements, and approval processes. The feature must display base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and deductions — but what each of those terms means, how they are calculated, and what must legally be shown varies by jurisdiction.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Earnings & Compensation",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Earnings Breakdown", "definition": "An itemized decomposition of a driver's total compensation into its constituent components for a given period." },
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The guaranteed or minimum compensation component before bonuses, tips, or deductions. For employees (CA), this is an hourly wage subject to minimum wage floors. For contractors (ID), this is a per-task service fee." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation awarded per completed delivery, often based on distance, time, surge, or performance criteria." },
      { "term": "Tip", "definition": "A voluntary gratuity from the customer intended exclusively for the driver. Tips are never platform revenue and must never subsidize base pay." },
      { "term": "Deduction", "definition": "Any amount subtracted from gross earnings. Must be itemized with a reason code and, where legally required, pre-authorized by the driver." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal labor relationship — employee or independent contractor — which determines applicable wage laws, tax treatment, and display requirements." },
      { "term": "Itemized Wage Statement", "definition": "A legally mandated breakdown of earnings (California Labor Code §226) including gross wages, total hours, piece rates, deductions, net wages, and pay period." },
      { "term": "Works Council Approval (Betriebsrat-Zustimmung)", "definition": "Formal consent from the German works council required before deploying systems that affect compensation presentation to employees. A hard prerequisite, not a post-launch formality." },
      { "term": "Gross Earnings", "definition": "The sum of base pay + per-delivery bonus + tips, before any deductions are applied." },
      { "term": "Net Earnings", "definition": "Gross earnings minus all deductions. The amount the driver actually receives." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "The displayed total earnings must always equal the sum of all itemized components (base pay + bonuses + tips − deductions = net earnings). No rounding discrepancy or hidden residual is permitted.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Mathematical consistency of financial data. If the parts do not sum to the whole, the system is producing corrupt financial records.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every earnings breakdown displayed, assert: base_pay + per_delivery_bonus + tips - sum(deductions) == net_earnings, to the cent."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Tips must never be counted toward base pay, minimum wage calculations, or any platform-obligated compensation floor. Tips are pass-through funds belonging to the driver.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Violating this is illegal under California Labor Code §351 and is a fundamental breach of trust in all jurisdictions. If tips subsidize base pay, the system is misrepresenting compensation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that minimum wage compliance calculations exclude tip amounts entirely. Assert that base_pay >= applicable_minimum_wage_floor independent of tips."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, the earnings display must constitute a compliant itemized wage statement under Labor Code §226: gross wages earned, total hours worked, applicable hourly rate(s), piece-rate units and rates if applicable, all deductions with descriptions, net wages, pay period start and end dates, and employer identity.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Non-compliance with §226 creates statutory penalties per pay period per employee. This is a legal data-integrity requirement — omitting required fields makes the statement legally deficient.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any California driver, assert the earnings view contains all §226 required fields: gross_wages, hours_worked, hourly_rate, deductions_itemized, net_wages, pay_period_start, pay_period_end, employer_name, employer_address."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The feature must not be deployed to drivers in Germany until formal Betriebsrat approval has been obtained and recorded in the system.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Deploying compensation-display changes without works council approval violates the German Works Constitution Act (BetrVG §87). This is not a soft preference — it is a legal gate that, if bypassed, exposes the company to injunctions and labor court proceedings.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the feature flag for Germany earnings transparency cannot be activated unless a Betriebsrat approval record exists with status=approved and a date on or before the activation date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Every deduction must have an itemized reason code, a description, and — for California employees — prior written authorization on file, except for deductions required by law (taxes, garnishments).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Unauthorized deductions from employee wages violate California Labor Code §224. Displaying a deduction without a traceable authorization means the deduction itself may be illegal.",
      "testable_assertion": "For each deduction in a California driver's breakdown, assert: deduction has reason_code AND description AND (authorization_on_file == true OR deduction_type IN ['tax', 'court_garnishment', 'statutory'])."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The earnings breakdown must reflect the driver's legal classification in the jurisdiction. Employee drivers (CA, DE) see wage-framed language (wages, hours, overtime). Contractor drivers (ID) see service-fee-framed language (service fees, task earnings). The system must never use employee terminology for contractors or vice versa.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Using employee terminology for contractors creates legal evidence of misclassification. Using contractor terminology for employees undermines their legal protections. Either direction corrupts the legal relationship the system encodes.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the UI label set rendered for a given driver matches their jurisdiction's classification enum. California/Germany drivers never see 'service fee'; Indonesia drivers never see 'wages' or 'hourly rate'."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Earnings data must be displayed in the local currency of the driver's operating jurisdiction (USD for California, EUR for Germany, IDR for Indonesia).",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "While critical for usability and legal compliance, currency display is a configurable policy tied to jurisdiction mapping, not a data-integrity invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the currency code on the earnings breakdown matches the driver's jurisdiction currency: CA→USD, DE→EUR, ID→IDR."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Net earnings must never be displayed as negative without a clear, prominent explanation. If deductions exceed gross earnings in a period, the system must flag this as an exceptional state requiring review.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "A policy to protect driver trust and prevent confusion. A VP could adjust the threshold or handling, but the system should default to protective behavior.",
      "testable_assertion": "If net_earnings < 0, assert the UI renders an 'exceptional deduction' alert and the record is flagged for payroll review."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "California drivers must see overtime earnings as a separate line item when hours exceed daily (>8h) or weekly (>40h) thresholds, at the legally mandated multiplier.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Under AB5, drivers are employees entitled to overtime. Failing to separately display overtime earnings would make the wage statement non-compliant with §226 and mask potential wage theft.",
      "testable_assertion": "For California drivers with hours > 8 in a day or > 40 in a week, assert an 'overtime_pay' line item exists with rate >= 1.5x the regular hourly rate."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "The 'real-time' earnings display must reflect earnings data no more stale than the platform's settlement cycle (e.g., upon delivery completion). Tips that arrive asynchronously must be appended to the breakdown when received, with a visible timestamp.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The exact staleness threshold is a product policy, not a data-integrity invariant. But the principle — that displayed data must track actual events — is essential for transparency.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that within T seconds of a delivery completion event, the earnings breakdown includes the delivery's base pay and bonus. Assert that late-arriving tips appear with a timestamp indicating when they were added."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver in California completes a delivery",
      "when": "They view their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see an itemized statement with gross wages, hours worked, hourly rate, overtime (if applicable), per-delivery bonus, tips (separate from base), all deductions with descriptions, net wages, and pay period — all in USD, using employee wage terminology"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "The Betriebsrat has NOT approved the earnings transparency feature",
      "when": "The platform attempts to enable the feature for German drivers",
      "then": "The system refuses activation and returns an error referencing missing works council approval"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The Betriebsrat HAS approved the feature and the approval is recorded",
      "when": "A German driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see a compliant breakdown in EUR using employee wage terminology, with all deductions itemized"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver in Indonesia completes a delivery",
      "when": "They view their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see service fee, per-delivery bonus, tips, and any deductions — in IDR, using independent contractor terminology (no 'wages', 'hourly rate', or 'overtime')"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A California driver has worked 10 hours in a single day",
      "when": "They view their daily earnings",
      "then": "The breakdown shows 8 hours at regular rate and 2 hours at ≥1.5x overtime rate as a separate line item"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A customer tips a driver 30 minutes after delivery completion",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown after the tip arrives",
      "then": "The tip appears as a new line item with a timestamp, and the total is recalculated correctly"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A driver in any jurisdiction views their earnings",
      "when": "They add up all displayed components (base + bonus + tips − deductions)",
      "then": "The sum exactly equals the displayed net earnings with zero discrepancy"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A California driver has a platform-initiated deduction (e.g., equipment rental)",
      "when": "The deduction appears in the earnings breakdown",
      "then": "It shows a reason code, description, and the system has a corresponding written authorization record on file"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A driver has zero completed deliveries in a pay period but logged on-duty hours (California)", "expected_behavior": "California employees must still see minimum wage for hours worked with zero bonuses/tips. The breakdown shows base pay for hours at minimum hourly rate, zero delivery bonuses, zero tips." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple deductions exceed gross earnings, resulting in negative net pay", "expected_behavior": "Display the negative net amount with a prominent alert, flag the record for payroll review, and do not auto-settle until reviewed. Never silently carry forward a negative balance." },
    { "scenario": "A tip is disputed or reversed after being displayed in the breakdown", "expected_behavior": "Show the reversal as an explicit negative line item with a 'tip adjustment' reason code and timestamp. Never silently remove a previously displayed tip." },
    { "scenario": "German works council approval is revoked after feature is live", "expected_behavior": "The system must support deactivation of the feature for German drivers. The Betriebsrat approval gate must be re-evaluable, not a one-time check." },
    { "scenario": "A driver operates in multiple jurisdictions (e.g., cross-border in EU)", "expected_behavior": "Each delivery's earnings must be calculated and displayed under the rules of the jurisdiction where the delivery was performed. The breakdown must not blend classification rules across jurisdictions." },
    { "scenario": "Currency conversion is needed (e.g., a German driver views historical earnings from before EUR adoption — unlikely, but: platform credits in different currency)", "expected_behavior": "Display in the driver's jurisdiction currency. If conversion occurred, show the original amount, exchange rate, and converted amount as separate fields." },
    { "scenario": "System cannot retrieve real-time earnings data (service outage)", "expected_behavior": "Display the last known breakdown with a clear 'as of [timestamp]' indicator. Never show stale data without a staleness warning. Never show an empty or partial breakdown without explanation." },
    { "scenario": "Indonesia reclassifies drivers as employees (regulatory change)", "expected_behavior": "The system must support jurisdiction-level classification changes that update terminology, display requirements, and calculation rules without requiring a full feature rebuild." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Non-compliance with California §226 carries penalties of $50 for the first violation and $100 per subsequent violation per employee per pay period, uncapped in class actions. Germany works council violations can result in injunctions halting the entire feature. Correct implementation builds driver trust, reducing costly churn (driver acquisition costs $300-$1500 per driver in competitive markets).",
    "operational": "Three distinct jurisdiction configurations must be maintained. The Betriebsrat approval gate introduces a deployment dependency that cannot be parallelized with engineering work for Germany. Real-time earnings calculation adds load to settlement systems. Terminology configuration per jurisdiction requires a localization and legal review pipeline.",
    "user": "Drivers gain visibility into how their pay is calculated, directly increasing trust and reducing support tickets about pay discrepancies. California drivers receive a legally mandated right. Indonesian contractors get transparency as a competitive differentiator. German drivers must wait until works council approves.",
    "risk": "HIGH legal risk if California wage statement requirements are incomplete — class action exposure. HIGH compliance risk if Germany feature is deployed without Betriebsrat approval. MEDIUM misclassification risk if terminology bleeds across jurisdiction boundaries. LOW risk in Indonesia due to fewer regulatory constraints, but reputational risk if the feature is perceived as less transparent there.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (California)", "impact": "Receive legally required wage transparency. Incorrect implementation directly harms their statutory rights and creates grounds for individual or class action claims." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Germany)", "impact": "Cannot access the feature until works council approves. Must be clearly communicated to avoid perception of being deprioritized." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Indonesia)", "impact": "Receive transparency as a goodwill/competitive feature. Terminology must not inadvertently imply employment relationship." },
      { "group": "Legal & Compliance Team", "impact": "Must review and sign off on jurisdiction-specific display templates. Ongoing obligation to update rules as regulations change." },
      { "group": "German Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "impact": "Has co-determination rights. Must be engaged early in the process — not presented with a fait accompli." },
      { "group": "Payroll & Finance", "impact": "Earnings breakdowns become a driver-facing audit trail. Any discrepancy between what is displayed and what is paid becomes a discoverable liability." },
      { "group": "Product & Engineering", "impact": "Must build a jurisdiction-aware, classification-aware display system — not a one-size-fits-all screen. Significant complexity increase." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive driver inquiries about displayed earnings. Must be trained on jurisdiction-specific rules and terminology." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a single sentence covering three jurisdictions with fundamentally different legal frameworks. California AB5 and §226 requirements are well-established but the spec does not confirm the platform's actual compliance posture. Germany's Betriebsrat requirement is a hard blocker that the spec does not address at all — this is a critical missing context. Indonesia's contractor model is simpler but the spec provides no detail on local labor law nuances. No legal review is referenced. The confidence is low because shipping this feature incorrectly in California creates class-action exposure, and the Germany blocker could invalidate the entire rollout timeline.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Tips must not subsidize base pay — universal principle, legally codified in CA",
      "Arithmetic integrity of earnings breakdowns — fundamental financial invariant",
      "Betriebsrat approval is a hard prerequisite for Germany — well-established German labor law",
      "Driver classification must dictate terminology — misclassification risk is well-documented"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact §226 field requirements — need legal counsel to confirm which fields apply to gig-economy wage statements vs traditional employment",
      "Germany-specific display requirements beyond Betriebsrat approval — German wage transparency laws may impose additional field requirements",
      "Indonesia local labor law nuances — spec assumes no requirements but local regulations on contractor payment transparency may exist",
      "Overtime calculation edge cases — California daily and weekly overtime interact in complex ways (double-time after 12 hours, 7th consecutive day rules)",
      "Whether 'real-time' creates legal obligations around data accuracy SLAs"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "California drivers are classified as employees under AB5 with no applicable exemption",
      "The platform operates the Betriebsrat relationship and has an established works council — not a greenfield engagement",
      "Indonesia has no current legal mandate for earnings transparency, but this should be verified with local counsel",
      "The platform's settlement system can produce itemized earnings data at the per-delivery level",
      "Tips are always customer-to-driver; the platform does not pool or redistribute tips"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No legal review is referenced in the spec. California §226 compliance, German BetrVG requirements, and Indonesian contractor payment laws each require jurisdiction-specific legal sign-off. Defining acceptance criteria without legal counsel risks encoding incorrect legal requirements.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal & Compliance", "Drivers (all jurisdictions)", "Executive leadership (liability exposure)"],
      "options": [
        "Proceed with best-effort legal interpretation and flag for legal review before implementation",
        "Block domain analysis until legal review of all three jurisdictions is complete"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Proceed with current analysis as a starting framework but mandate legal review of the acceptance criteria before any implementation begins. California and Germany criteria should be treated as draft until counsel confirms."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec says 'real-time' but does not define the latency SLA. In California, there may be a legal distinction between 'real-time display' and 'official wage statement.' If the real-time view is treated as the wage statement, it must meet §226 requirements at all times. If it's an informal preview, different rules apply.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (California)", "Legal & Compliance", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "Treat the real-time view as the official itemized wage statement (higher compliance burden, simpler UX)",
        "Treat it as an informal preview with a separate official wage statement delivered per pay period (lower real-time compliance burden, two systems to maintain)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Treat the real-time view as an informal preview and maintain a separate compliant §226 wage statement per pay period. This reduces the real-time accuracy SLA while maintaining legal compliance. But legal counsel must confirm this interpretation."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec lists 'deductions' without specifying which deduction types exist. Employee deductions (CA: taxes, benefits, garnishments) are fundamentally different from contractor deductions (ID: platform fees, service charges). The domain model for 'deduction' must be jurisdiction-aware.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (all)", "Payroll & Finance", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "Define a single polymorphic Deduction entity with jurisdiction-specific subtypes",
        "Define separate deduction models per classification (EmployeeDeduction, ContractorDeduction)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Use a single Deduction entity with a required classification_context field and jurisdiction-specific validation rules. This keeps the domain model unified while enforcing that employee deductions and contractor deductions are never conflated."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
